checks.question: Roughly 96% of the human body is made of just four elements. Name them and explain in one sentence why this is evidence that biology is chemistry.
checks.answer: Oxygen, carbon, hydrogen, and nitrogen. Living systems are built from the same atoms found elsewhere in nature — life is a particular arrangement of ordinary chemistry, not a separate kind of matter.
checks.misconception_tested: thinking chemistry is somehow separate from biology rather than the substrate biology runs on

checks.skill: match each macromolecule to its monomer and main function
checks.question: For each macromolecule, give the monomer and the main biological function: carbohydrate, lipid, protein, nucleic acid.
checks.answer: Carbohydrate: monomer is a monosaccharide (e.g., glucose); function is quick energy and plant structure. Lipid: built from fatty acids + glycerol; function is long-term energy storage and forming membranes. Protein: monomer is an amino acid; function is enzymes, structure, transport, and signaling. Nucleic acid: monomer is a nucleotide; function is storing and reading instructions (DNA and RNA).
checks.misconception_tested: memorizing macromolecule names without locking the monomer and function to each name

checks.skill: explain why water's polarity matters for life
checks.question: Water is polar. Give two consequences of that polarity that directly support life inside cells or whole organisms.
checks.answer: Examples include: water dissolves nutrients and ions so cells can move materials; water sticks to itself (cohesion) so plants can pull water upward; water absorbs heat slowly so body temperature stays stable; water can be a reactant in building or breaking polymers. Any two valid consequences are accepted.
checks.misconception_tested: thinking of water as a passive background liquid rather than an active life-supporting molecule

checks.skill: tell dehydration synthesis from hydrolysis by tracking water
checks.question: A cell joins two amino acids into a dipeptide and releases one water molecule. Is that dehydration synthesis or hydrolysis? Then: a digestive enzyme adds a water molecule to cut a starch into smaller sugars. Which is that?
checks.answer: Joining amino acids and releasing water = dehydration synthesis (building a polymer). Using water to split starch into sugars = hydrolysis (breaking a polymer). Rule: water released → build; water consumed → break.
checks.misconception_tested: confusing dehydration synthesis and hydrolysis because both involve water and a polymer

checks.skill: predict the direction of a polymer reaction from the chemistry
checks.question: An enzyme reaction shows H₂O on the reactant side of an arrow involving a long polymer. Will the polymer get longer or shorter? Justify your answer.
checks.answer: Shorter. Water on the reactant side means water is being consumed to split a bond, which is hydrolysis. Hydrolysis breaks a polymer into smaller pieces, so the chain gets shorter.
checks.misconception_tested: ignoring where the water sits in the equation when deciding whether a polymer is being built or broken
